2.1: When the day of Pentecost arrived, they were all together in one place.
2.2: Suddenly a sound like the blowing of a strong wind came from heaven and filled the whole house where they were sitting.
2.3: And they saw what seemed to be tongues of fire that separated and came to rest on each of them.
2.4: They were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues as the Holy Spirit enabled them.
2.5: Now, among the Jews residing in Jerusalem, there were devout men from every nation under heaven.
2.6: At the sound that was heard, they came running in a crowd and were quite beside themselves, because each one heard them speaking their own language.
2.7: Surprised and astonished, they said, "Aren't all these people who are speaking Galileans?"
2.8: How is it that we hear them each speaking in their native language?
2.9: All of us, Parthians, Medes, Elamites, inhabitants of Mesopotamia, Judea and Cappadocia, Pontus and Asia,
2.10: from Phrygia and Pamphylia, from Egypt and the regions of Libya near Cyrene, Romans passing through here,
2.11: Whether Jews or proselytes, Cretans and Arabs, we hear them proclaiming the wonders of God in our own languages.»
2.12: They were all astonished and, not knowing what to think, they said to each other, "What could it possibly be?"«
2.13: Others mockingly said, "They are full of new wine."«
2.14: Then Peter, standing up with the Eleven, raised his voice and said to them, «Jews and all of you who live in Jerusalem, let me explain this to you and listen carefully to what I say:
2.15: These men are not drunk, as you suppose, because it is the third hour of the day.
2.16: What you see is what was foretold by the prophet Joel:
2.17: «In the last days,” says the Lord, “I will pour out my Spirit on all people. Your sons and daughters will prophesy, your young men will see visions, and your old men will dream dreams.”.
2.18: Yes, in those days I will pour out my Spirit on my servants, both men and women, and they will prophesy.
2.19: And I will show wonders in the heavens above and signs on the earth below: blood, fire, and swirling smoke,
2.20: The sun will turn into darkness and the moon into blood, before the coming of the great and glorious day of the Lord.
2.21: And whoever calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.
2.22: Children of Israel, listen to these words: Jesus of Nazareth, a man attested to you by God with wonders, miracles, and signs that he performed through him among you, as you yourselves know,
2.23: This man was handed over according to God’s unchanging plan and foreknowledge, and you crucified him and put him to death by the hands of lawless men.
2.24: God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the pains of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.
2.25: For David said of him, «I kept the Lord always before me, because he is at my right hand, so that I would not be shaken.
2.26: Therefore my heart rejoices and my tongue exults, and my body also will rest in hope.,
2.27: for you will not leave my soul in the realm of the dead, nor will you allow your Holy One to see corruption.
2.28: You have shown me the paths of life, and you will fill me with joy by showing me your face.»
2.29: My brothers, let me tell you frankly about the patriarch David, that he died, that he was buried, and that his tomb is still among us today.
2.30: Because he was a prophet and knew that God had sworn to him to place a son of his own blood on his throne,
2.31: It is the resurrection of Christ that he foresaw, saying that his soul would not be left in the realm of the dead and that his flesh would not see corruption.
2.32: This is the Jesus whom God resurrected, we are all witnesses to it.
2.33: And now that he has been taken up to heaven at the right hand of God and has received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this Spirit which you see and hear.
2.34: For David did not ascend into heaven, but he himself said, «The Lord said to my Lord, ‘Sit at my right hand,’”,
2.35: until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.»
2.36: Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly that God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Christ.»
2.37: Their hearts pierced by this speech, they said to Peter and the other apostles, «Brothers, what shall we do?»
2.38: Peter answered them, «Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit.
2.39: For the promise is for you, for your children, and for all who are far off—for all whom the Lord our God will call.»
2.40: And with many other words he urged and exhorted them, saying, «Save yourselves from the midst of this perverse generation.»
2.41: Those who received Peter's message were baptized, and that day about three thousand people were added to their number.
2.42: They devoted themselves to the preaching of the Apostles, to the common meetings, to the breaking of bread, and to prayer.
2.43: And fear was in every soul, and many wonders and miracles were performed by the Apostles.
2.44: All those who believed lived together and had everything in common.
2.45: They sold their lands and possessions and shared the proceeds among everyone, according to each person's needs.
2.46: Every day, all together, they attended the temple and, breaking their bread in their homes, they ate their food with joy and simplicity.,
2.47: praising God and having the favor of all the people. And the Lord added daily to the number of those who were in the way of salvation.

3.1: Peter and John went up together to the temple for the ninth hour prayer.
3.2: Now, there was a man, lame from birth, who was carried around. He was placed every day near the temple gate, called the Beautiful Gate, so that he could ask for alms from those who entered the temple.
3.3: This man, having seen Peter and John about to enter, asked them for alms.
3.4: Peter and John both stared at him and said, "Look at us."«
3.5: He looked at them attentively, expecting to receive something from them.
3.6: But Peter said to him, «I have neither gold nor silver, but what I do have I give you: In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk.»
3.7: And taking him by the hand, he helped him to his feet. At that very moment, his legs and feet became firm,
3.8: He leaped to his feet and began to walk. Then he entered with them into the temple, walking, jumping, and praising God.
3.9: All the people saw him walking and praising God.
3.10: And recognizing that it was the very same man who used to sit at the Beautiful Gate of the temple asking for alms, they were astonished and beside themselves at what had happened to him.
3.11: As he did not leave Peter and John, all the astonished people ran towards them, to the portico called Solomon's.
3.12: Seeing this, Peter said to the people, «Children of Israel, why are you amazed at this? And why do you stare at us as though by our own power or piety we had made this man walk?
3.13: The God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, the God of your fathers, has glorified his servant Jesus, whom you handed over and denied before Pilate, even though he had decided to release him.
3.14: You have denied the Holy and the Just and have sought mercy for a murderer.
3.15: You killed the Author of life, whom God raised from the dead, and we are witnesses of this.
3.16: It is because of the faith received from him that his name has strengthened the man whom you see and know; it is the faith that comes from him that has worked this perfect healing before all of you.
3.17: I know, brothers, that you acted in ignorance, as did your leaders.
3.18: But in this way God fulfilled what he had foretold through all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer.
3.19: Repent, then, and turn to God, so that your sins may be wiped out.,
3.20: so that times of refreshing may come from the Lord, and that he may send the one appointed for you, Jesus Christ,
3.21: that heaven must receive until the days of the restoration of all things, days which God spoke of long ago through the mouth of his holy prophets.
3.22: Moses said, "The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers; you shall listen to him in everything he tells you.
3.23: And whoever does not listen to this prophet will be cut off from the people.»
3.24: All the prophets who have spoken since Samuel have also foretold these days.
3.25: You are the children of the prophets and of the covenant that God made with your fathers when he said to Abraham, «Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed.»
3.26: It is to you first that God, having raised up his Son, sent him to bless you, when each of you turns away from your iniquities.

4.1: While Peter and John were speaking to the people, the priests, the captain of the temple guard, and the Sadducees arrived.,
4.2: dissatisfied with what they were teaching the people and announcing in the person of Jesus the resurrection of the dead.
4.3: They seized them and threw them into prison until the next day, for it was already evening.
4.4: However, many of those who had heard this speech believed, and the number of men rose to about five thousand.
4.5: The next day, their leaders, the Elders and the Scribes, assembled in Jerusalem,
4.6: with Annas, the high priest, Caiaphas, John, Alexander and all those who were of the papal family.
4.7: And having brought the Apostles before them, they asked them, «By what power or in whose name did you do this?»
4.8: Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, said to them:
4.9: «Leaders of the people and elders of Israel: if we are asked today about a good deed done to a disabled person, how this man was healed,
4.10: Know this well, all of you and all the people of Israel: It is by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified and whom God raised from the dead, it is by him that this man stands before you fully healed.
4.11: This Jesus is the stone that you rejected from the building and which has become the cornerstone.
4.12: And salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved.»
4.13: When they saw the boldness of Peter and John, knowing that they were uneducated, ordinary men, they were astonished, and at the same time recognized them as having been with Jesus.
4.14: But, as they saw the man who had been healed standing near them, they had nothing to reply.
4.15: Having brought them out of the Sanhedrin, they began to deliberate among themselves, saying:
4.16: «What shall we do to these men? That they have performed a remarkable miracle is evident to all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, and we cannot deny it.
4.17: But to prevent this matter from spreading further among the people, let us forbid them, with threats, from speaking in that name to anyone whatsoever.»
4.18: And having summoned them, they absolutely forbade them to speak or teach in the name of Jesus.
4.19: Peter and John answered them, «Judge for yourselves whether it is right in God’s sight to obey you rather than God.
4.20: For us, we cannot remain silent about what we have seen and heard.»
4.21: So they threatened them and released them, not knowing how to punish them because of the people, since everyone was glorifying God for all that had just happened.
4.22: For the man who had been healed in such a miraculous way was over forty years old.
4.23: Once freed, they went to their brothers and told them everything that the Princes of the priests and the Elders had told them.
4.24: When the brothers heard this, they all raised their voices to God, saying, «Sovereign Lord, you are the one who made the heavens and the earth and the sea and everything in them.
4.25: It is you who said [by the Holy Spirit], through the mouth of [our father] David, your servant: «Why did the nations tremble and the peoples form vain plots?
4.26: The kings of the earth have risen up, the princes have conspired against the Lord and against his Christ.»
4.27: Truly, in this city, Herod and Pontius Pilate, together with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel, have conspired against your holy servant, Jesus, whom you have anointed.,
4.28: to do what your hand and your advice had decided in advance.
4.29: And now, Lord, consider their threats and grant to your servants to proclaim your word with full boldness,
4.30: by stretching out your hand so that healings, miracles, and wonders may be performed, through the name of your holy servant Jesus.»
4.31: When they had prayed, the place where they were meeting was shaken. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the word of God boldly.
4.32: The multitude of the faithful had but one heart and one soul; no one called what he possessed his own, but everything was held in common among them.
4.33: With great power the apostles bore witness to the resurrection of the Savior Jesus, and great grace was upon them all.
4.34: for there was no one needy among them: all those who owned land or houses sold them
4.35: and brought the prize money to the feet of the Apostles, and it was then distributed to each person according to their needs.
4.36: A Levite from Cyprus, Joseph, nicknamed Barnabas by the apostles, which translates as Son of Consolation,
4.37: He owned a field, sold it, brought the money and laid it at the feet of the Apostles.

5.1: But a man named Ananias, along with his wife Sapphire, sold a property,
5.2: and having, in concert with her, retained some of the prize money, he brought the rest and laid it at the feet of the Apostles.
5.3: Peter said to him, «Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit and to keep back some of the price of this field?
5.4: Couldn't you have kept it without selling it, and after selling it, weren't you still in control of the money? How could you have conceived such a plan? You haven't lied to men, but to God.»
5.5: Upon hearing these words, Ananias fell down and died, and all who heard of it were filled with great fear.
5.6: The young men got up, wrapped the body, and carried it away to be buried.
5.7: About three hours later, Ananie's wife came in, not knowing what had happened.
5.8: Pierre asked her, "Tell me, is this the price you sold your field for?" "Yes," she replied, "that's the price."«
5.9: Then Peter said to him, «How could you agree to test the Spirit of the Lord? Look, the feet of the young men who buried your husband are striking the threshold; they are going to bury you also.»
5.10: At that very moment, she fell at the Apostle's feet and died. The young men entered and found her dead; they carried her out and buried her beside her husband.
5.11: Great fear spread throughout the church and among all who learned of this event.
5.12: Many miracles and wonders were performed among the people by the hands of the apostles. And they were all together in Solomon's Portico,
5.13: No one else dared to join them, but the people praised them highly.
5.14: Each day saw an increase in the number of men and women who believed in the Lord,
5.15: so that the sick were brought into the streets and placed on beds or mats, so that when Peter passed by, at least his shadow would cover some of them.
5.16: People came in droves from the neighboring towns to Jerusalem, bringing sick people and those tormented by unclean spirits, and all were healed.
5.17: Then the high priest and all those who were with him, namely the party of the Sadducees, rose up, filled with jealousy.
5.18: And having arrested the Apostles, they threw them into a public prison.
5.19: But an angel of the Lord, having opened the prison doors during the night, brought them out, saying:
5.20: «Go, stand in the temple and proclaim to the people all these words of life.»
5.21: Having heard this, they went early in the morning into the temple and began to teach. Meanwhile, the high priest and all those with him gathered together, assembled the council and all the elders of the children of Israel, and sent to the prison to bring them in.
5.22: The guards went and, not finding them in the prison, returned and made their report, saying:
5.23: «"We found the prison securely locked and the guards standing in front of the gates, but after opening them, we found no one inside."»
5.24: When the high priest, the commander of the temple, and the chief priests heard these words, they were greatly perplexed about the prisoners, not knowing what they could be.
5.25: At that moment someone came to them and said, «Those whom you had put in prison are now in the temple courts teaching the people.»
5.26: The commander immediately surrendered with his men and brought them in without using violence, because they feared being stoned by the people.
5.27: Having brought them in, they presented them before the Sanhedrin, and the high priest questioned them, saying:
5.28: «We expressly forbade you to teach that name, and yet you have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine and now you want to bring this man’s blood upon us.»
5.29: Peter and the Apostles replied: "We must obey God rather than men.
5.30: The God of our fathers raised Jesus from the dead, whom you had killed by hanging him on a tree.
5.31: God exalted him to his right hand as Prince and Savior, to give repentance to Israel and forgiveness of sins.
5.32: And we are witnesses of these things, along with the Holy Spirit whom God has given to those who obey him.»
5.33: Exasperated by what they had just heard, the council members were of the opinion that they should be put to death.
5.34: But a Pharisee named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law, revered by all the people, stood up in the Sanhedrin and ordered that the Apostles be put outside for a moment,
5.35: He said, «Children of Israel, be careful what you do to these men.
5.36: For not long ago Theodas appeared, who presented himself as a personage, and about four hundred men attached themselves to him: he was killed and all those who had followed him were scattered and reduced to nothing.
5.37: After him arose Judas the Galilean, at the time of the census, and he attracted people to his party: he too perished, and all his followers were scattered.
5.38: Here is my advice to you: Pay no more attention to these people and let them go. If this idea or this work originates from men, it will destroy itself.,
5.39: But if it comes from God, you cannot destroy it. Do not risk having fought against God himself.»
5.40: They gave in to his opinion and having called the Apostles back, they had them flogged, then they forbade them to speak in the name of Jesus and released them.
5.41: The Apostles left the Sanhedrin, rejoicing that they had been judged worthy to suffer disgrace for the name of Jesus.
5.42: And every day, in the temple and from house to house, they did not cease to proclaim Jesus as the Christ.

6.1: In those days, as the number of disciples increased, the Hellenists raised complaints against the Hebrews, because their widows were neglected in the daily care.
6.2: Then the Twelve, having gathered together the multitude of disciples, said to them, «It is not fitting that we should neglect the word of God in order to wait on tables.
6.3: Therefore, brothers, choose from among you seven men of good reputation, full of the Holy Spirit and wisdom, to whom we may entrust this office,
6.4: And we will devote ourselves entirely to prayer and to the ministry of the word.»
6.5: This speech pleased the whole assembly and they elected Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas and Nicolas, a proselyte from Antioch.
6.6: They were presented to the apostles, and after praying, the apostles laid their hands on them.
6.7: The word of God spread more and more, the number of disciples increased greatly in Jerusalem, and a multitude of priests became obedient to the faith.
6.8: Stephen, full of grace and power, performed wonders and great miracles among the people.
6.9: Some members of the synagogue known as the Synagogue of the Freedmen and of the Synagogue of the Cyrenians and Alexandrians, along with Jews from Cilicia and Asia, came to argue with him,
6.10: but they could not resist the wisdom and the Spirit with which he spoke.
6.11: So they bribed some people who said, "We heard him utter blasphemous words against Moses and against God."«
6.12: They thus roused the people, the Elders and the scribes and together they rushed upon him, seized him and dragged him to the Sanhedrin.
6.13: And they produced false witnesses, who said, «This man never stops speaking against the holy place and against the Law.
6.14: For we have heard him say that Jesus, this Nazarene, will destroy this place and change the institutions that Moses gave us.»
6.15: As all those who sat in the council had their eyes fixed on Stephen, his face appeared to them like that of an angel.

7.1: The high priest asked him, "Is that really the case?"«
7.2: Stephen replied, «My brothers and fathers, listen. The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham while he was still in Mesopotamia, before he came to live in Haran,
7.3: and said to him, «Leave your country and your family and go to the land I will show you.»
7.4: So he left the land of the Chaldeans and settled in Haran. From there, after the death of his father, God caused him to emigrate to this land where you now live.
7.5: And he gave him no property in that country, not even a place to set foot, but he promised him, at a time when the patriarch had no children, to give possession of it to him and his posterity after him.
7.6: God spoke thus: «His descendants will dwell in foreign lands, and they will be enslaved and mistreated for four hundred years.
7.7: »But I will judge the nation that held them in slavery,” says the Lord. “After that, they will come out and serve me in this place.”
7.8: Then he gave Abraham the covenant of circumcision, and so Abraham, after fathering Isaac, circumcised him on the eighth day. Isaac fathered and circumcised Jacob and Jacob, the twelve patriarchs.
7.9: Driven by jealousy, the patriarchs sold Joseph to be taken to Egypt. But God was with him.,
7.10: and he delivered him from all his trials and gave him grace and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, who put him at the head of Egypt and of all his house.
7.11: Now a famine struck all the land of Egypt and Canaan. The distress was great, and our ancestors could find nothing to eat.
7.12: Jacob, having learned that there was food in Egypt, sent our fathers there for the first time.
7.13: And the second time, Joseph was recognized by his brothers and Pharaoh knew what his origin was.
7.14: Then Joseph sent for his father Jacob and all his family, consisting of seventy-five people.
7.15: And Jacob went down to Egypt, where he died, as did our fathers.
7.16: And they were carried to Shechem and placed in the tomb that Abraham had bought with money from the sons of Hamor in Shechem.
7.17: As the time drew near for the fulfillment of the promise God had sworn to Abraham, the people increased and multiplied in Egypt,
7.18: until another king appeared in that country who had not known Joseph.
7.19: This king, using trickery against our race, mistreated our fathers, to the point of making them expose their children so that they would not live.
7.20: At that time Moses was born, who was beautiful in the sight of God; he was nursed for three months in his father’s house.
7.21: And when he had been exposed, Pharaoh's daughter took him in and raised him as her son.
7.22: Moses was instructed in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was powerful in word and deed.
7.23: When he reached the age of forty, it came to him to visit his brothers, the children of Israel.
7.24: He saw one being insulted, and taking up his defense, he avenged the oppressed by killing the Egyptian.
7.25: He thought that his brothers would understand that God was granting them deliverance through his hand, but they did not understand.
7.26: The next day, having met two who were fighting, he urged them to peace, saying, "Men, you are brothers: why mistreat one another?"«
7.27: But the one who was mistreating his neighbor rejected him, saying, «Who made you a leader and judge over us?”
7.28: "Do you want to kill me, like you killed the Egyptian yesterday?"»
7.29: At this word, Moses fled and went to live in the land of Midian, where he fathered two sons.
7.30: Forty years later, in the desert of Mount Sinai, an angel appeared to him in the flame of a burning bush.
7.31: At this sight, Moses was filled with amazement, and as he approached to examine it, the voice of the Lord spoke to him:
7.32: «"I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob." And Moses, trembling, did not dare to look.
7.33: Then the Lord said to him, «Take off your sandals, for the place where you are walking is holy ground.
7.34: »I have seen the affliction of my people in Egypt, I have heard their groaning, and I have come down to deliver them. Now come, and I will send you to Egypt.”
7.35: This is the Moses whom they had denied, saying, "Who appointed you ruler and judge?" This is the one whom God sent as ruler and deliverer, with the assistance of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.
7.36: It was he who brought them out, performing wonders and miracles in the land of Egypt, in the Red Sea, and in the desert for forty years.
7.37: This is the Moses who said to the children of Israel: "God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers [listen to him]."«
7.38: It was he who, in the midst of the assembly, in the desert, with the angel who spoke to him on Mount Sinai, and with our fathers, received living oracles to transmit to us.
7.39: Our fathers, far from wanting to obey him, rejected him and, returning in their hearts to Egypt,
7.40: They said to Aaron, "Make us gods who will go before us, for as for this Moses who brought us out of the land of Egypt, we do not know what has become of him."«
7.41: They then made a golden calf and offered a sacrifice to the idol and rejoiced in the work of their hands.
7.42: But God turned away and gave them over to the worship of the army of heaven, as it is written in the book of the Prophets: «Did you offer me sacrifices and offerings for forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?
7.43: You have carried the tent of Moloch and the star of your god Raiphan, these images that you made to worship. Therefore, I will carry you off beyond Babylon.»
7.44: Our fathers in the desert had the tabernacle of the testimony, as commanded by the one who told Moses to build it according to the pattern he had seen.
7.45: Having received it from Moses, our fathers brought it, under the leadership of Joshua, when they conquered the land from the nations that God drove out before them, and it remained there until the days of David.
7.46: This king found favor with God and asked to build a dwelling place for the God of Jacob.
7.47: Nevertheless, it was Solomon who built him a temple.
7.48: But the Most High does not dwell in temples made by human hands, according to the word of the prophet:
7.49: «Heaven is my throne and the earth is my footstool. What dwelling place will you build for me, says the Lord, or what will be the place of my rest?”
7.50: "Wasn't it my hand that did all these things?"»
7.51: You stubborn men, uncircumcised in heart and ears, you always resist the Holy Spirit, as were your fathers, so are you.
7.52: What prophet did your ancestors not persecute? They even killed those who foretold the coming of the Righteous One, and today you have betrayed him and put him to death.
7.53: You who received the Law, in consideration of the angels who commanded you, and you did not keep it.»
7.54: Upon hearing these words, rage tore at their hearts and they gnashed their teeth at him.
7.55: But Stephen, who was filled with the Holy Spirit, looked up to heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of his Father.
7.56: And he said, «Look, I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God.»
7.57: The Jews then shouted loudly, covering their ears, and all rushed at him together.
7.58: And having led him out of the city, they stoned him. The witnesses laid their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul.
7.59: While they were stoning him, Stephen prayed, saying, «Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.»
7.60: Then, kneeling down, he cried out in a loud voice, «Lord, do not hold this sin against them.» After saying this, he fell asleep [in the Lord]. Now Saul had approved of Stephen’s murder.

8.1: On the same day, a violent persecution broke out against the Church in Jerusalem and all except the apostles were scattered throughout the countryside of Judea and Samaria.
8.2: Pious men buried Stephen and mourned greatly over him.
8.3: And Saul ravaged the church, entering houses, dragging men and women out and throwing them into prison.
8.4: Those who were scattered went throughout the land proclaiming the word.
8.5: Philip went down to a city in Samaria and preached Christ there.
8.6: And the crowds paid attention to what Philip said, as they learned and saw the miracles he performed.
8.7: For unclean spirits, crying out with loud voices, came out of many who were possessed; many who were paralyzed or lame were also healed.,
8.8: And there was great joy in that city.
8.9: But there was already a man there named Simon, who practiced magic and amazed the people of Samaria, presenting himself as a great personage.
8.10: Everyone, young and old, had become attached to him. This man, they said, is the Virtue of God, the one that is called the Great.
8.11: They were therefore attached to him because, for a long time, he had seduced them with his enchantments.
8.12: But when they believed Philip, who proclaimed to them the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women.
8.13: Simon himself believed and, having been baptized, he attached himself to Philip and the miracles and great wonders of which he was awestruck him.
8.14: The Apostles, who were in Jerusalem, having heard that Samaria had received the word of God, sent Peter and John there.
8.15: When they arrived at the Samaritans' house, they prayed for them, that they might receive the Holy Spirit.
8.16: For he had not yet come down on any of them; they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.
8.17: Then Peter and John laid their hands on them, and they received the Holy Spirit.
8.18: When Simon saw that the Holy Spirit was given through the laying on of hands by the Apostles, he offered them money,
8.19: saying, «Give me this power also, so that everyone on whom I lay my hands may receive the Holy Spirit.»
8.20: But Peter said to him, «May your money perish with you, because you thought that the gift of God could be obtained with money.
8.21: You have absolutely no part in this favor, because your heart is not pure before God.
8.22: Therefore, repent of your iniquity and pray to the Lord to forgive you, if possible, the thought of your heart.
8.23: For I see that you are in bitter gall and in the bonds of sin.»
8.24: Simon replied, "Pray to the Lord yourselves for me, so that nothing that you have said may happen to me."«
8.25: As for them, after bearing witness and preaching the word of the Lord, they returned to Jerusalem, proclaiming the good news in many Samaritan villages.
8.26: An angel of the Lord spoke to Philip, saying, «Get up and go south to the road that leads down from Jerusalem to Gaza.» (This is a desert road.)»
8.27: He got up and left. And behold, an Ethiopian, a eunuch, minister of Candace, queen of Ethiopia and superintendent of all her treasures, had come to Jerusalem to worship.
8.28: He would return and, sitting on a chariot, he would read the prophet Isaiah.
8.29: The Spirit said to Philip, «Go up and stand beside that chariot.»
8.30: Philip ran up and, hearing the Ethiopian reading the prophet Isaiah, he said to him, "Do you understand what you are reading?"«
8.31: He replied, "How could I, unless someone guides me?" And he asked Philip to come up and sit with him.
8.32: But the passage of Scripture he was reading was this: «Like a sheep he was led to the slaughter, and like a lamb before its shearer is silent, so he did not open his mouth.
8.33: It was in his humiliation that his judgment was sealed. As for his generation, who will tell of it? For his life was cut off from the earth.»
8.34: The eunuch said to Philip, "Please, who is the prophet talking about? Himself or someone else?"«
8.35: Then Philip opened his mouth and, beginning with this passage, told him about Jesus.
8.36: On their way they came to some water, and the eunuch said, "Here is water: what can prevent me from being baptized?"«
8.37: [Philip replied, "If you believe with all your heart, it is possible." "I believe," the eunuch replied, "that Jesus Christ is the Son of God."]
8.38: So he had his chariot stopped and Philip, going down with him into the water, baptized him.
8.39: When they came out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord suddenly took Philip away, and the eunuch did not see him again, but went on his way rejoicing.
8.40: As for Philip, he found himself in Azotus, from where he went to Caesarea, evangelizing all the towns through which he passed.

9.1: However, Saul, still breathing the threat and fear of death against the Lord's disciples, went to the high priest
9.2: and asked him for letters to the synagogues of Damascus, so that if he found people of that faith, men or women, he would bring them in chains to Jerusalem.
9.3: As he was on his journey and approaching Damascus, suddenly a light from heaven shone around him.
9.4: He fell to the ground and heard a voice saying to him, "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?"«
9.5: He answered, «Who are you, Lord?» And the Lord said, «I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. [It is not good for you to kick against the goad.]»
9.6: Trembling and terrified, he said, «Lord, what do you want me to do?» The Lord answered him, «Get up and go into the city, and there you will be told what you must do.»
9.7: The men who were with him were filled with amazement, for they could hear the sound of the voice, but could see no one.
9.8: Saul got up from the ground, and although his eyes were open, he could see nothing; they took him by the hand and led him to Damascus.,
9.9: And he was there for three days without seeing anything and without taking any food or drink.
9.10: Now there was a disciple in Damascus named Ananias. The Lord said to him in a vision, «Ananias.» He replied, «Here I am, Lord.»
9.11: And the Lord said to him, «Get up and go to the street called Straight, and inquire at the house of Judas for a man named Saul of Tarsus, for he is praying.»
9.12: And he saw in a vision a man named Ananias, who came in and laid his hands on him so that he might regain his sight.
9.13: Ananias replied, «Lord, I have heard from many about all the harm this man has done to your saints in Jerusalem.
9.14: And he has here, from the chief priests, full authority to bind with chains all those who invoke your name.»
9.15: But the Lord said to him, «Go, for this man is my chosen instrument to carry my name before the Gentiles and kings and the children of Israel,
9.16: And I will show him all that he must suffer for my name.»
9.17: Ananias went away and, having arrived at the house, laid his hands on Saul, saying, «Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus, who appeared to you on the road as you were coming here, has sent me so that you may regain your sight and be filled with the Holy Spirit.»
9.18: At that very moment, something like scales fell from Saul's eyes, and he regained his sight. He stood up and was baptized.,
9.19: And after he had eaten, his strength returned. Saul spent a few days with the disciples who were in Damascus.,
9.20: And immediately he began to preach in the synagogues that Jesus is the Son of God.
9.21: All who heard it were amazed and said, «Isn’t this the same man who persecuted those who call on this name in Jerusalem? And hasn’t he come here to lead them in chains to the chief priests?»
9.22: However, Saul felt his courage redouble and he confounded the Jews of Damascus, demonstrating to them that Jesus is the Christ.
9.23: After a considerable amount of time, the Jews formed a plan to kill him.,
9.24: But their plot came to Saul's attention. The gates were guarded day and night, in order to put him to death.
9.25: But the disciples took him during the night and lowered him down through the wall in a basket.
9.26: He went to Jerusalem and sought to make contact with the disciples, but they all feared him, not being able to believe that he was a disciple of Jesus.
9.27: Then Barnabas took him with him and brought him to the Apostles and told them how on the road Saul had seen the Lord, who had spoken to him, and with what courage he had preached the name of Jesus in Damascus.
9.28: From then on, Saul went back and forth with them in Jerusalem and spoke boldly in the name of the Lord.
9.29: He also addressed the Hellenists and argued with them, but they sought to kill him.
9.30: The brothers, having learned of this, took him to Caesarea, from where they sent him to Tarsus.
9.31: The church was at peace throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria, growing and progressing in the fear of the Lord and multiplying through the help of the Holy Spirit.
9.32: Now it happened that Peter, visiting the saints from city to city, went down to those who lived in Lydda.
9.33: There he found a man named Aeneas, lying on a bed for eight years: he was a paralytic.
9.34: Peter said to him, "Aeneas, Jesus Christ heals you, get up and make your own bed." And immediately he got up.
9.35: All the inhabitants of Lydda and Sharon saw him and they turned to the Lord.
9.36: In Joppa, among the disciples, there was a woman named Tabitha, in Greek Dorcas: she was rich in good works and gave alms generously.
9.37: She fell ill at that time and died. After washing her, they placed her in an upper room.
9.38: Since Lydda is near Joppa, the disciples, having learned that Peter was there, sent two men to him with this plea: «Do not delay in coming to us.»
9.39: Peter got up and went with them. As soon as he arrived, he was led into the upper room and all the widows surrounded him, weeping and showing him the tunics and garments that Dorcas had made while she was with them.
9.40: Peter sent everyone out, knelt down and prayed, then, turning towards the corpse, he said: "Tabitha, get up." She opened her eyes and, having seen Peter, she sat up.
9.41: Peter reached out his hand to her and helped her to her feet. Then he called together the saints and the widows and presented her to them alive.
9.42: This miracle became known throughout the city of Joppa, and a great number of people believed in the Lord.
9.43: Pierre stayed for some time in Joppa, at the home of a tanner named Simon.

10.1: In Caesarea there lived a man named Cornelius, a centurion in the Italian cohort,
10.2: Religious and God-fearing, as was his entire household, he gave much alms to the people and prayed to God constantly.
10.3: In a vision, around the ninth hour of the day, he clearly saw an angel of God who entered his house and said to him:
10.4: «"Cornelius." Fixing his eyes on the angel and seized with terror, he cried out: "What is it, Lord?" The angel answered him: "Your prayers and your alms have ascended before God as a memorial.
10.5: And now send men to Joppa and bring back a certain Simon, surnamed Peter,
10.6: He is staying with a tanner named Simon, whose house is located near the sea.»
10.7: When the angel who had been speaking to him had left, Cornelius called two of his servants and a pious soldier from among those who were attached to his person,
10.8: And after telling them everything, he sent them to Joppa.
10.9: The next day, as the messengers were on their way and approaching the city, Peter went up on the roof at about the sixth hour to pray.
10.10: Then, feeling hungry, he wanted to eat. While his meal was being prepared, he fell into a state of ecstasy:
10.11: He saw the sky opened and something descending from it, like a large sheet, tied at its four corners and coming down towards the earth,
10.12: Inside were all the four-legged animals and reptiles of the earth and the birds of the sky.
10.13: And a voice said to him, "Get up, Peter, kill and eat."«
10.14: Peter replied, "Oh no, Lord, for I have never eaten anything profane or unclean."«
10.15: And a voice spoke to him again: «What God has declared pure, do not call profane.»
10.16: This was done three times and immediately afterwards the sheet was lifted into the sky.
10.17: Now Peter was searching within himself for the meaning of the vision he had had, and behold, the men sent by Cornelius, having inquired about Simon's house, came to the gate,
10.18: and having called out, they asked if this was where Simon, surnamed Peter, was staying.
10.19: And as Peter was reflecting on the vision, the Spirit said to him, «Here are three men looking for you.
10.20: "Get up, go downstairs and go with them without fear, for it is I who have sent them."»
10.21: Immediately Peter went down to them: "I am," he said, "the one you are looking for. What is the reason that brings you here?"«
10.22: They replied, "Cornelius the centurion, a righteous and God-fearing man, commended by the whole Jewish nation, was instructed by a holy angel to send for you to come to his house and listen to your words."«
10.23: So Peter brought them in and lodged them. The next day, having gotten up, he left with them, and some of the brothers from Joppa accompanied him.
10.24: They entered Caesarea the following day. Cornelius was waiting for them and had invited his relatives and close friends.
10.25: When Peter entered, Cornelius went to meet him and fell at his feet and prostrated himself.
10.26: But Peter helped him up, saying, "Get up, I too am a man."«
10.27: And while he was talking with him, he went in and found a large crowd of people gathered.
10.28: He said to them, «You know that it is forbidden for a Jew to associate with a foreigner or to enter his house, but God has shown me not to call any man defiled or unclean.
10.29: So I came without hesitation, as soon as you sent for me. I would therefore ask you to tell me the reason why you summoned me.»
10.30: Cornelius replied: "For four days now I have been fasting and praying in my house at the ninth hour, when suddenly a man dressed in a dazzling robe appeared before me, who said to me:
10.31: «"Cornelius, your prayer has been answered and God has remembered your alms.
10.32: Send to Joppa and call for Simon, who is called Peter. He is staying at the house of Simon the tanner by the sea. [He will come and speak to you.]»
10.33: I immediately sent word to you, and you did well to come. Now we are all gathered before God to hear everything God has commanded you to tell us.»
10.34: Then Peter opened his mouth and spoke thus: «Truly I acknowledge that God is impartial,
10.35: But in every nation, he who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him.
10.36: He sent the word to the children of Israel, announcing peace through Jesus Christ: he is Lord of all.
10.37: You know what happened throughout Judea, beginning with Galilee, after the baptism that John preached:
10.38: how God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power, and he went around doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil, because God was with him.
10.39: For us, we are witnesses of everything he did in the countryside of Judea and in Jerusalem. Then they put him to death by hanging him on a tree.
10.40: But God raised him from the dead on the third day and enabled him to be seen,
10.41: not to all the people, but to the witnesses chosen beforehand by God, to us who ate and drank with him after his resurrection from the dead.
10.42: And he commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that this is the one whom God has appointed as judge of the living and the dead.
10.43: All the prophets testify about him that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name.»
10.44: Peter was still speaking when the Holy Spirit came upon all who heard the word.
10.45: The faithful who had come from the circumcision group and who accompanied Peter were beside themselves when they saw that the gift of the Holy Spirit had been poured out even on the Gentiles.
10.46: For they heard them speaking in tongues and praising God. Then Peter said:
10.47: «"Can we refuse baptismal water to these men who have received the Holy Spirit just as we have?"»
10.48: And he commanded that they be baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ. After which they asked him to stay a few days.

11.1: The apostles and the brothers who were in Judea learned that the Gentiles also had received the word of God.
11.2: And when Peter returned to Jerusalem, the circumcised believers reproached him,
11.3: saying, "You went into the house of uncircumcised men and ate with them."«
11.4: Pierre, taking the floor, began to explain to them, in a coherent manner, what had happened.
11.5: «I was praying,” he said, “in the city of Joppa, and I had a vision in my ecstasy: something like a large sheet, held by its four corners, was coming down from heaven and coming toward me.”.
11.6: Fixing my eyes on this cloth, I considered it and saw the four-footed animals of the earth, the wild beasts, the reptiles, and the birds of the air.
11.7: I also heard a voice saying to me: "Get up, Peter, kill and eat.
11.8: I replied, "Oh no, Lord, for nothing profane or impure has ever entered my mouth.
11.9: For the second time a voice was heard from heaven: What God has declared pure, do not call profane.
11.10: This happened three times, then everything was lifted up into the sky.
11.11: At that very moment, three men appeared in front of the house where we were; they had been sent from Caesarea to me.
11.12: The Spirit told me to go with them without hesitation. These six brothers accompanied me, and we entered Cornelius's house.
11.13: This man told us how he had seen the angel appear to him in his house, saying: Send to Joppa and bring back Simon, called Peter,
11.14: He will speak words to you by which you and all your household will be saved.
11.15: When I began to speak to them, the Holy Spirit descended upon them, as upon us at the beginning.
11.16: And I remembered this saying of the Lord: John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.
11.17: If then God gave them the same grace he gave us who believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to be able to stand in God's way?»
11.18: Having heard this, they calmed down and glorified God, saying, "So God has granted repentance to the Gentiles, so that they may have life."«
11.19: However, those who had been scattered by the persecution that arose on account of Stephen went as far as Phoenicia, the island of Cyprus, and Antioch, proclaiming the word to no one except the Jews.
11.20: However, some men from Cyprus and Cyrene came to Antioch and also spoke to the Greeks, proclaiming the Lord Jesus to them.
11.21: And the hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number of people believed and turned to the Lord.
11.22: When the news reached the ears of the faithful of the Church in Jerusalem, they sent Barnabas to Antioch.
11.23: When he arrived and saw the grace of God, he rejoiced and urged them all to remain steadfast in the Lord.
11.24: For he was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and of faith. And a considerable crowd was joined to the Lord.
11.25: Barnabas then went to Tarsus to look for Saul and having found him, he brought him to Antioch.
11.26: Now it came to pass that for a whole year they held meetings in that church and taught a great multitude. It was thus in Antioch that the disciples were first called Christians.
11.27: In those days, prophets came from Jerusalem to Antioch.
11.28: One of them, named Agabus, stood up and announced by the Spirit that there would be a great famine throughout the earth; indeed, it took place during the reign of Claudius.
11.29: The disciples decided to send aid, each according to their means, to the brothers who lived in Judea:
11.30: which they did. This aid was sent to the Elders by the hands of Barnabas and Saul.

12.1: Around that time, King Herod had some members of the Church arrested and mistreated.,
12.2: He had James, John's brother, put to death by the sword.
12.3: Seeing that this pleased the Jews, he also ordered Peter's arrest: this was during the days of Unleavened Bread.
12.4: When he had him in his power, he threw him into prison and put him under the guard of four squads of four soldiers each, with the intention of bringing him before the people after Passover.
12.5: While Peter was thus kept in prison, the Church continued to offer prayers to God for him.
12.6: But on the very night of the day when Herod was to bring him to trial, Peter, bound with two chains, was sleeping between two soldiers, and sentries were guarding the prison at the gate.
12.7: Suddenly an angel of the Lord appeared and a light shone in the prison. The angel struck Peter on the side and woke him, saying, «Get up quickly!» and the chains fell off his hands.
12.8: The angel said to him, "Put on your belt and sandals." He did so, and the angel added, "Wrap yourself in your cloak and follow me."«
12.9: Peter went out and followed him, not knowing that what was being done by the angel was real; he believed he was having a vision.
12.10: When they had passed the first guard, then the second, they arrived at the iron gate which leads into the city: it opened of its own accord before them, they went out and entered a street and immediately the angel left him.
12.11: Then Peter came to his senses and said, «Now I see that the Lord has really sent his angel and has rescued me from Herod’s hand and from everything the Jewish people were expecting.»
12.12: After some time to reflect, he went to the house of Mary, the mother of John, surnamed Mark, where a large assembly was praying.
12.13: He knocked on the vestibule door and a servant named Rhode approached to listen.
12.14: As soon as she recognized Peter's voice, in her joy, instead of opening the door, she ran inside to announce that Peter was at the door.
12.15: They told her, "You're crazy." But she insisted that it was so, and they said, "It's her angel."«
12.16: However, Peter kept knocking, and when they opened the door, seeing him, they were filled with amazement.
12.17: But Peter, having motioned with his hand for them to be silent, told them how the Lord had brought him out of prison and he added: «Go and tell James and the brothers.» Then he left and went to another place.
12.18: When day broke, there was great commotion among the soldiers, as they wondered what had become of Peter.
12.19: Herod had him searched for and, not finding him, proceeded to interrogate the guards and had them led to their execution. Then he left Judea to return to Caesarea, where he stayed.
12.20: Herod was at war with the Tyrians and the Sidonians, who came together to him and, having won over Blastus, his chamberlain, they asked him for peace, because their country drew its sustenance from the king's lands.
12.21: On the appointed day, Herod, dressed in royal robes and seated on his throne, addressed them,
12.22: And the people cried out, "This is the voice of a God, not of a man!"«
12.23: At that very moment, an angel of the Lord struck him down, because he had not given glory to God. And he died, eaten by worms.
12.24: However, the word of God spread more and more and gave birth to new disciples.
12.25: Barnabas and Saul, having completed their ministry, returned from Jerusalem, taking with them John, surnamed Mark.

13.1: In the church at Antioch there were prophets and teachers, namely, Barnabas, Simeon called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen, who had been brought up with Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.
13.2: While they were serving the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit said to them, «Separate Saul and Barnabas for me from the work to which I have called them.»
13.3: So, after fasting and praying, they laid their hands on them and let them go.
13.4: Sent by the Holy Spirit, Saul and Barnabas went to Seleucia, from where they sailed for Cyprus.
13.5: When they arrived at Salamis, they proclaimed the word of God in the Jewish synagogues. John was with them to assist them in their ministry.
13.6: Having traveled all over the island as far as Paphos, they found a certain magician, a false Jewish prophet, named Barjesus,
13.7: who lived with the proconsul Sergius Paulus, a wise man. The latter, having summoned Barnabas and Saul, expressed a desire to hear the word of God.
13.8: But Elymas, the magician, for that is the meaning of his name, opposed them, seeking to turn the proconsul away from the faith.
13.9: Then Saul, also called Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, fixed his gaze on the magician,
13.10: He said to him, «You son of the devil, full of all kinds of deceit and trickery, enemy of all righteousness, will you not cease perverting the straight ways of the Lord?”
13.11: »Now behold, the hand of God is upon you, and you shall be blind, deprived for a time of the sight of the sun.” Immediately thick darkness fell upon him, and he looked about seeking someone to lay his hand upon him.
13.12: Upon seeing this marvel, the proconsul believed, deeply struck by the teachings of the Lord.
13.13: Paul and his companions embarked at Paphos, went to Perga in Pamphylia, but John left them and returned to Jerusalem.
13.14: They, going beyond Perga, came to Antioch of Pisidia and having entered the synagogue on the Sabbath day, they sat down.
13.15: After the reading of the Law and the Prophets, the leaders of the synagogue sent word to them, saying, «Brothers, if you have any exhortation to address to the people, speak.»
13.16: Paul stood up and, motioning with his hand, said: «Children of Israel and you who fear God, listen.
13.17: The God of this people of Israel chose our fathers. He glorified this people during their stay in Egypt and brought them out of it with his mighty arm.
13.18: For nearly forty years, he took care of it in the desert.
13.19: Then, having destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, he put him in possession of their territory.
13.20: After that, for about four hundred and fifty years, he gave him judges until the prophet Samuel.
13.21: So they asked for a king, and God gave them Saul, son of Cish, of the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years.
13.22: Then, having rejected him, he raised up David to be their king, to whom he gave this testimony: I have found David, son of Jesse, a man after my own heart, who will carry out all my wishes.
13.23: It is from his descendants that God, according to his promise, brought forth for Israel a Savior, Jesus.
13.24: Before his arrival, John had preached a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.,
13.25: And when he had finished his race, he said: I am not he whom you think I am, but behold, after me comes one whose sandal I am not worthy to untie.
13.26: My brothers, children of the race of Abraham and you who fear God, it is to you that this message of salvation has been sent.
13.27: For the inhabitants of Jerusalem and their officials, having rejected Jesus and the oracles of the prophets which are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by their judgment.,
13.28: And having found nothing in him that deserved death, they asked Pilate to put him to death.
13.29: And when they had fulfilled all that was written about him, they took him down from the cross and laid him in a tomb.
13.30: But God raised him from the dead, and for several days he appeared to those
13.31: who had gone up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem and who are now his witnesses to the people.
13.32: We also announce to you that the promise made to our fathers,
13.33: God has accomplished it for us, their children, by raising Jesus from the dead, according to what is written in the second Psalm: You are my Son, today I have begotten you.
13.34: That God raised him from the dead so that he will not return to corruption, is what he declared when he said: I will give you the divine favors promised to David, favors which are assured.
13.35: That is why he also says elsewhere: You will not allow your Holy One to see corruption.
13.36: Now David, after having fulfilled God's purposes while he lived, fell asleep and was gathered to his fathers and saw corruption.
13.37: But the one whom God resurrected did not see corruption.
13.38: Therefore, my brothers, know this: it is through him that the forgiveness of sins and of all defilements is proclaimed to you, for which you were not justified by the law of Moses,
13.39: Whoever believes is justified by Him.
13.40: Therefore, beware lest what is said in the Prophets happen to you:
13.41: »Look, you disdainful men, be astonished and perish, for I am going to do a work in your days, a work you would not believe if someone told you about it.”
13.42: When they left, they were asked to speak on the same subject the following Sabbath.
13.43: And after the assembly, many Jews and devout proselytes followed Paul and Barnabas, and they spoke with them and urged them to persevere in the grace of God.
13.44: The following Sabbath, almost the entire city gathered to hear the word of God.
13.45: When the Jews saw this crowd, they were filled with jealousy and blasphemed, contradicting everything Paul said.
13.46: Then Paul and Barnabas spoke out boldly, «It was necessary that the word of God should be spoken to you first; but since you reject it and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we are turning to the Gentiles.”
13.47: For the Lord has commanded us: »I have appointed you to be a light for the nations, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.”
13.48: When the Gentiles heard these words, they rejoiced and glorified the word of the Lord, and all who were destined for eternal life became believers.
13.49: And the word of the Lord spread throughout the whole land.
13.50: But the Jews, having stirred up the proselyte women of the upper class and the notables of the city, raised up a persecution against Paul and Barnabas and drove them out of their territory.
13.51: Then Paul and Barnabas shook the dust off their feet against them and went to Iconium.
13.52: However, the disciples were filled with joy and the Holy Spirit.

14.1: In Iconium, Paul and Barnabas also went into the Jewish synagogue and spoke there, and a great multitude of Jews and Greeks embraced the faith.
14.2: But the Jews who remained unbelieving stirred up and embittered the spirit of the Gentiles against their brothers.
14.3: Nevertheless, they stayed for quite a long time, speaking with confidence, supported by the Lord, who bore witness to the word of his grace, by the wonders and miracles which he enabled them to perform.
14.4: The whole city was divided; some were for the Jews, others for the Apostles.
14.5: But as the Gentiles and the Jews, with their leaders, moved to insult and stone them,
14.6: The Apostles, having learned of this, took refuge in the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra and Derbe, and the surrounding country.,
14.7: and they announced the good news there.
14.8: In Lystra there was a man crippled in his legs, who sat up because he was lame from birth and had never walked.
14.9: He was listening to Paul speak, and Paul, having fixed his eyes on him and seeing that he had faith to be healed, said in a loud voice, "Stand up straight on your feet." Immediately he jumped up and walked.
14.10: When the crowd saw what Paul had just done, they raised their voices, saying in the Lycaonian language, "The gods have come down to us in human form!"«
14.11: And they called Barnabas Jupiter and Paul Mercury, because it was he who spoke.
14.12: Moreover, the priest of the temple of Jupiter, who was at the entrance to the city, brought bulls with bandages before the gates and wanted, along with the crowd, to offer a sacrifice.
14.13: When the apostles Paul and Barnabas heard of this, they tore their clothes and rushed out into the crowd.
14.14: And in a resounding voice they said, «O men, why are you doing this? We too are men subject to the same weaknesses as you, we announce to you that you must leave these vanities and turn to the living God, who made the heaven and the earth, the sea and all that is in them.
14.15: This God, in past ages, allowed all nations to follow their own ways,
14.16: Yet he did not cease to bear witness to himself, doing good, sending rain from heaven and favorable seasons, giving us abundant food and filling our hearts with joy.»
14.17: Despite these words, they only with difficulty succeeded in preventing the people from offering them a sacrifice.
14.18: Then Jews came from Antioch and Iconium, won over the people, stoned Paul, and dragged him out of the city, thinking he was dead.
14.19: But the disciples surrounded him, and he got up and went back into the city. The next day he left for Derbe with Barnabas.
14.20: When they had evangelized that city and made a considerable number of disciples, they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch.,
14.21: strengthening the spirit of the disciples, exhorting them to persevere in the faith, and saying that it is through many tribulations that we must enter the kingdom of God.
14.22: They appointed elders in each church, after praying and fasting, and commended them to the Lord, in whom they had believed.
14.23: Afterwards, they crossed Pisidia and came to Pamphylia,
14.24: and after proclaiming the word of God in Perga, they went down to Attalia.
14.25: From there they embarked for Antioch, from whence they had departed, having been commended to the grace of God for the work which they had just accomplished.
14.26: As soon as they arrived, they gathered the church and recounted all that God had done for them and how he had opened the door of faith to the nations.
14.27: and they remained in Antioch for quite some time with the disciples.

15.1: Now some men came down from Judea and were teaching the brothers this doctrine: «Unless you are circumcised according to the law of Moses, you cannot be saved.»
15.2: Paul and Barnabas having therefore had a dispute and a heated discussion with them, it was decided that Paul and Barnabas, with some others of their number, should go up to Jerusalem to the Apostles and Elders to discuss this matter.
15.3: After being accompanied by the Church, they continued their journey through Phoenicia and Samaria, recounting the conversion of the Pagans, which caused great joy to all the brothers.
15.4: When they arrived in Jerusalem, they were received by the Church, the Apostles and the Elders, and they recounted all that God had done for them.
15.5: Then some of the party of the Pharisees, who had believed, stood up and said that the Gentiles should be circumcised and commanded to observe the law of Moses.
15.6: The Apostles and Elders gathered to examine this matter.
15.7: After a long discussion, Peter stood up and said to them, «Brothers, you know that God chose me from among you long ago so that through my mouth the Gentiles might hear the word of the gospel and believe
15.8: And God, who knows the hearts, testified in their favor, giving them the Holy Spirit just as he gave him to us,
15.9: He made no distinction between them and us, having purified their hearts by faith.
15.10: Why then are you testing God now, by placing on the disciples a yoke that neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear?
15.11: But it is through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ that we believe we are saved, just as they are.»
15.12: The whole assembly remained silent and listened to Barnabas and Paul, who recounted all the miracles and wonders that God had done through them among the Gentiles.
15.13: When they had finished speaking, James spoke up and said, «Brothers, listen to me.
15.14: Simon recounted how God first took care to draw from among the Gentiles a people who would bear his name.
15.15: The words of the prophets agree with this purpose, as it is written:
15.16: After that I will return and rebuild the tent of David that lies fallen on the ground; I will repair its ruins and raise it up.,
15.17: so that the rest of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the nations that are called by my name, says the Lord, who does these things.
15.18: The work of the Lord is known from all eternity.
15.19: That is why I believe that we should not disturb those among the Gentiles who convert to God.
15.20: Let them simply be told that they must abstain from idol defilements, sexual sins, strangled meats, and blood.
15.21: For Moses has had preachers in every city for generations, since he is read in the synagogues every Sabbath day.»
15.22: Then it seemed good to the Apostles and the Elders, together with the whole Church, to choose some of their number to send to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas; they chose Judas, surnamed Barsabbas, and Silas, prominent figures among the brothers.
15.23: They entrusted them with a letter worded as follows: "The Apostles, the Elders and the brothers, to the brothers among the Gentiles who are in Antioch, Syria and Cilicia: Greetings.
15.24: Having learned that some of our people have come, without any mandate from us, to disturb you with speeches that have upset your souls,
15.25: We have gathered together and decided it appropriate to choose delegates and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,
15.26: these men who risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.
15.27: We therefore have MP Jude and Silas, who will tell you the same things in person.
15.28: It seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us not to impose any burden on you beyond what is necessary, namely:
15.29: "Abstain from food offered to idols, blood, strangled meat, and sexual sins. By keeping yourselves from these things, you will do well. Farewell."»
15.30: Having therefore taken leave, the delegates went to Antioch, assembled all the faithful and gave them the letter.
15.31: It was read aloud and everyone was happy with the consolation it contained.
15.32: Judah and Silas, who were themselves prophets, addressed the brothers many times, encouraging and strengthening them.
15.33: After a stay of some time, they were dismissed by the brothers, with wishes of peace towards those who had sent them.
15.34: However, Silas decided it was best to stay, and Jude went to Jerusalem alone.
15.35: Paul and Barnabas remained in Antioch, teaching and proclaiming the word of the Lord with many others.
15.36: After a few days, Paul said to Barnabas, «Let us go back and visit the brothers in the various towns where we preached the word of the Lord, to see how they are doing.»
15.37: Barnabas also wanted to take John, nicknamed Mark,
15.38: But Paul thought it best not to take as a companion a man who had left them from Pamphylia and who had not been working with them.
15.39: This disagreement was such that they separated from each other, and Barnabas took Mark and sailed with him to Cyprus.
15.40: Paul chose Silas and left, commended by the brothers to the grace of God.
15.41: He travelled through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches.

16.1: Paul then went to Derbe, and then to Lystra. There was a disciple there named Timothy, the son of a Jewish Christian woman and a Greek father.
16.2: The brothers from Lystra and Iconium spoke highly of him.
16.3: Paul wanted to take him with him and, having taken him, he circumcised him, because of the Jews who were in those regions, for they all knew that his father was a Greek.
16.4: As they traveled through the cities, they taught the faithful to observe the decisions of the Apostles and Elders of Jerusalem.
16.5: And the churches were strengthened in the faith and grew daily.
16.6: When they had traveled through Phrygia and the region of Galatia, the Holy Spirit prevented them from preaching the word in Asia,
16.7: They arrived at the borders of Mysia and were preparing to enter Bithynia, but the Spirit of Jesus did not allow them to do so.
16.8: So, having quickly crossed Mysia, they went down to Troas.
16.9: During the night, Paul had a vision: a Macedonian man stood before him and begged him, «Come over to Macedonia and help us.»
16.10: After this vision of Paul, we immediately sought to go to Macedonia, certain that God was calling us to proclaim the good news there.
16.11: Having therefore set sail from Troas, we sailed straight towards Samothrace and the next day we landed at Neapolis.
16.12: From there we went to Philippi, which is the first city in this part of Macedonia and a colony. We stayed a few days in this city.
16.13: On the Sabbath day, we went outside the gate to the bank of a river, where we thought the place of prayer was. We sat down and spoke to the women who had gathered there.
16.14: Now in the audience was a woman named Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira. She was a God-fearing woman, and the Lord opened her heart to pay attention to what Paul was saying.
16.15: When she and her family had been baptized, she addressed this prayer to us: "If you have judged that I have faith in the Lord, come into my house and stay there" and she compelled us by her entreaties.
16.16: One day as we were going to prayer, we met a young slave girl who had a Python spirit and brought great profit to her masters through her divinations.
16.17: She began to follow Paul and us, shouting, "These men are servants of the Most High God, who are telling you the way to salvation."«
16.18: She did this for several days. When Paul was distressed by it, he turned and said to the spirit, "I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her." And it came out at once.
16.19: The girl's owners, seeing their hopes of gain vanish, seized Paul and Silas and dragged them to the agora before the magistrates.
16.20: And having brought them before the magistrates, they said, «These men are disturbing our city. They are Jews,
16.21: They preach practices that we Romans are neither permitted to receive nor to follow.»
16.22: At the same time the crowd rose up against them and the magistrates, having torn off their clothes, ordered that they be whipped with sticks.
16.23: After they had been severely beaten, they had them put in prison, recommending to the jailer to guard them securely.
16.24: Having received this order, the jailer put them in one of the inner dungeons and placed their feet in stocks.
16.25: Around midnight, Paul and Silas were singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them.
16.26: Suddenly there was such a violent earthquake that the foundations of the prison shook; at the same instant, all the doors opened and the chains of all the prisoners fell away.
16.27: The jailer, having awakened and seeing the prison doors open, drew his sword and was about to kill himself, thinking that the prisoners had escaped.
16.28: But Paul shouted in a loud voice, "Don't hurt yourself, we're all here."«
16.29: Then the jailer, having asked for light, rushed in and, trembling, fell down at the feet of Paul and Silas,
16.30: Then he brought them out and said, «Sirs, what must I do to be saved?»
16.31: They replied, "Believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved, you and your household."«
16.32: And they spoke the word of God to him and to all who were in the house.
16.33: Taking them with him at that hour of the night, he washed their wounds and immediately afterwards he and all his family were baptized.
16.34: Then he brought them up into his house and served them food, rejoicing with his whole family that they had believed in God.
16.35: When day broke, the magistrates sent the guards who said, "Release these men."«
16.36: The jailer informed Paul: "The magistrates have sent orders to release you; come out now and go in peace."«
16.37: But Paul said to the guards, «They publicly flogged us, Roman citizens, without trial, and threw us into prison; now they want to release us secretly. Not so! Let them come themselves and set us free.»
16.38: The guards reported these words to the magistrates, who were frightened to learn that these men were Romans.
16.39: So they came and exhorted them and set them free, asking them to leave the city.
16.40: After leaving the prison, Paul and Silas went to Lydia's house and after seeing and encouraging the brothers, they left.

17.1: Having then passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, Paul and Silas arrived at Thessalonica, where the synagogue of the Jews was.
17.2: As was his custom, Paul went in and for three Sabbaths he reasoned with them. Starting from the Scriptures,
17.3: He explained and established that the Messiah had to suffer and rise from the dead, and "this Messiah," he said, "is Christ Jesus, whom I proclaim to you."«
17.4: Some Jews were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas, as well as a large number of God-fearing Gentiles and quite a number of leading women.
17.5: But the Jews, consumed by jealousy, recruited some wicked individuals from the dregs of the population, incited a mob, and stirred up unrest in the city. Then, rushing to Jason's house, they sought Paul and Silas to bring them before the people.
17.6: Not having found them, they dragged Jason and some brothers before the magistrates, shouting: «These men who have turned the world upside down have also come here,
17.7: And Jason received them. They are all in violation of Caesar's edicts, saying that there is another king, Jesus.»
17.8: They thus stirred up the people and the magistrates who were listening to them.
17.9: And it was only after receiving a guarantee from Jason and the others that they let them go.
17.10: The brothers, without wasting any time, sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea. When they arrived in that city, they went to the Jewish synagogue.
17.11: These latter had nobler sentiments than those of Thessalonica; they received the word with great eagerness, examining the Scriptures every day to see if what they were being taught was true.
17.12: Many of them, and among the Greeks, many high-ranking women and a large number of men, embraced the faith.
17.13: But when the Jews of Thessalonica learned that Paul was also proclaiming the word of God in Berea, they came there again to agitate the population.
17.14: So the brothers immediately sent Paul away to the sea, but Silas and Timothy remained in Berea.
17.15: Those who were driving Paul accompanied him as far as Athens, then, instructed to ask Silas and Timothy to come and join him as soon as possible, they returned.
17.16: While Paul was waiting for them in Athens, he felt in his soul a deep indignation at the sight of this city full of idols.
17.17: So he discussed in the synagogue with the Jews and the God-fearing men, and every day in the Agora with those he met.
17.18: Now, some Epicurean and Stoic philosophers having debated with him, some said: "What does this sower of words want from us?" Others, hearing him preach about Jesus and the resurrection, said: "It seems he has come to announce foreign deities to us."«
17.19: And having taken him with them, they brought him to the Areopagus, saying, "May we know what this new teaching is that you are teaching?
17.20: Because you're telling us strange things, we'd like to know what's going on.»
17.21: Now all the Athenians and foreigners living in the city spent their time doing nothing but telling or listening to news.
17.22: Paul, standing in the middle of the Areopagus, spoke thus: «Athenians, I perceive that in every respect you are exceedingly religious.
17.23: For when I passed by and looked at the objects of your worship, I even found an altar with this inscription: TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. The one you worship without knowing, I come to proclaim to you.
17.24: The God who made the world and everything in it, being Lord of heaven and earth, does not dwell in temples made by human hands,
17.25: He is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything, he who gives to all life, breath and all things.
17.26: From one man he brought forth all mankind to populate the face of the whole earth, having determined for each nation the duration of its existence and the boundaries of its territory,
17.27: so that men may seek him and find him as if groping, though he is not far from any one of us,
17.28: for it is in him that we have life, movement and being, and, as some of your own poets have also said: we are of his race.
17.29: Since we are of God's race, we must not believe that divinity is like gold, silver, or stone, sculpted by the art and genius of man.
17.30: God, having disregarded those times of ignorance, now commands all people, everywhere, to repent.,
17.31: For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed and accredited to all, by raising him from the dead.»
17.32: When they heard about the resurrection of the dead, some of them scoffed, while others said, "We'll hear you about that another time."«
17.33: So Paul withdrew from among them.
17.34: Some people, however, attached themselves to him and believed; among them were Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

18.1: After that, Paul left Athens and went to Corinth.
18.2: There he found a Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus, who had recently arrived from Italy with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had ordered all Jews to leave Rome. Paul went to see them,
18.3: And since he practiced the same trade, he stayed with them and worked there: they were tentmakers.
18.4: Every Sabbath, he would speak in the synagogue and persuade Jews and Greeks.
18.5: When Silas and Timothy arrived from Macedonia, he devoted himself entirely to preaching, testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Christ.
18.6: But when they opposed him and reviled him, Paul shook out his clothes and said to them, «Your blood be on your own heads! I am innocent of it; from now on I will go to the Gentiles.»
18.7: And leaving there, he went to the house of a man named Justus, a God-fearing man, whose house was next to the synagogue.
18.8: Crispus, the synagogue leader, believed in the Lord with his whole household, and a great number of Corinthians, when they heard Paul, also believed and were baptized.
18.9: During the night, the Lord said to Paul in a vision: «Do not be afraid, but speak and do not be silent.”
18.10: for I am with you, and no one will lay a hand on you to harm you, for I have many people in this city.»
18.11: Paul stayed in Corinth for a year and six months, teaching the word of God.
18.12: Now, since Gallio was proconsul of Achaea, the Jews unanimously rose up against Paul and brought him before the tribunal.,
18.13: saying, "This man persuades men to worship contrary to the Law."«
18.14: As Paul opened his mouth to reply, Gallio said to the Jews, "If it were a matter of some crime or serious wrongdoing, I would listen to you as is only reasonable, O Jews.
18.15: But since these are discussions about doctrine, names, and your law, that's your business; I don't want to be a judge of those things.»
18.16: And he sent them away from the court.
18.17: Then they all seized Sosthenes, the leader of the synagogue, and beat him in front of the tribunal, without Gallio paying any attention.
18.18: Paul stayed in Corinth for quite some time, and then, having said goodbye to the brothers, he embarked for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila, after having his head shaved at Cenchreae, in fulfillment of a vow.
