18.19: He arrived in Ephesus and left his companions there. He himself entered the synagogue and conversed with the Jews,
18.20: who begged him to extend his stay. But he did not consent.
18.21: And he took leave of them, saying, "[I must by all means keep the coming feast in Jerusalem.] I will return to you, God willing." And he departed from Ephesus.
18.22: Having landed at Caesarea, he went up to Jerusalem, greeted the Church and went down to Antioch.
18.23: After spending some time there, Paul set out and traveled successively through the country of the Galatians and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples.
18.24: Now a Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, an eloquent man well versed in the Scriptures, came to Ephesus.
18.25: He had been instructed in the way of the Lord and, with an ardent heart, he taught accurately about Jesus, although he knew only the baptism of John.
18.26: He began to speak boldly in the synagogue. Priscilla and Aquila, having heard him, took him aside and explained to him the way of the Lord more fully.
18.27: And since he wanted to go to Achaia, the brothers approved of him and wrote to the disciples to welcome him. When he arrived, he was of great help to those who had believed through grace.,
18.28: for he vigorously refuted the Jews in public, demonstrating from the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ.

19.1: Now, while Apollos was in Corinth, Paul, having traveled through the hill country, arrived in Ephesus. Having met some disciples,
19.2: He said to them, «Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?» They answered him, «We have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit.»
19.3: «Then what baptism did you receive?» Paul asked. They said, «John’s baptism.»
19.4: Paul then said, "John baptized with a baptism of repentance, telling the people to believe in the one who was coming after him, that is, in Jesus."«
19.5: Having heard these words, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.
19.6: When Paul laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came upon them and they began to speak in tongues and to prophesy.
19.7: There were about twelve of them in total.
19.8: Then Paul entered the synagogue and for three months spoke there with great boldness, persuasively discussing matters concerning the kingdom of God.
19.9: But, as some remained hardened and unbelieving, denouncing the way of the Lord before the people, he separated himself from them, took the disciples aside, and discoursed daily in the school of a man named Tyrannus.
19.10: He did this for two years, so that all who lived in Asia, both Jews and Greeks, heard the word of the Lord.
19.11: And God was performing extraordinary miracles through Paul,
19.12: to the point that handkerchiefs and belts that had touched his body were applied to the sick, and the illnesses left them and the evil spirits were driven out.
19.13: Some of the Jewish exorcists who roamed the country also tried to invoke the name of the Lord Jesus over those who had evil spirits, saying, "I adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preaches."«
19.14: Now there were seven sons of Sceva, the Jewish high priest, who engaged in this practice.
19.15: The evil spirit answered them, "I know Jesus and I know who Paul is, but who are you?"«
19.16: And the man who was possessed by the evil spirit threw himself upon them, overpowered them, and mistreated them so badly that they fled from that house naked and wounded.
19.17: When this happened, all the Jews and Greeks living in Ephesus were informed, and fear fell on them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was glorified.
19.18: A large number of those who had believed came to confess and declare their actions.
19.19: And among those who had indulged in superstitious practices, many brought their books and burned them before all the people: when estimating the value of these books, they found fifty thousand pieces of silver:
19.20: so powerful was the word of the Lord, spreading rapidly and proving its strength.
19.21: After this, Paul resolved to go to Jerusalem, passing through Macedonia and Achaia. "After I have been there," he said to himself, "I must also see Rome."«
19.22: He sent two of his assistants, Timothy and Erastus, to Macedonia, and he himself remained in Asia for some time.
19.23: At that time a great uproar arose concerning the Way of the Lord.
19.24: A goldsmith named Demetrius made small temples of Diana out of silver and provided his workers with a considerable profit.
19.25: Having gathered them together, along with others of the same trade, he said to them: «My friends, you know that our well-being depends on this industry,
19.26: And you see and hear that not only in Ephesus, but also in almost all Asia, this Paul has persuaded and turned away a great many people, saying that gods made with hands are not gods.
19.27: It is therefore to be feared not only that our industry will fall into disrepute, but also that the temple of the great goddess Diana will be held in contempt and even that the majesty of her, revered by Asia and the entire world, will be reduced to nothing.»
19.28: At these words, overcome with anger, they began to shout: "Great is Diana of the Ephesians!"«
19.29: Soon the city was filled with confusion. They all went together to the theatre, bringing with them Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians, who had accompanied Paul on his journey.
19.30: Paul wanted to enter the crowd, but the disciples prevented him.
19.31: Some of the Asiarchs, who were his friends, even sent messages to him, urging him not to appear at the theatre.
19.32: A thousand different cries could be heard, for disorder reigned in the assembly and most did not know why they had gathered.
19.33: Then Alexander, whom the Jews were pushing forward, was pulled from the crowd. He gestured with his hand that he wanted to speak to the people.
19.34: But when they realized that he was Jewish, they all shouted with one voice for nearly two hours: "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"«
19.35: The city secretary, having finally calmed the crowd, said: "Ephesians, what man does not know that the city of Ephesus is devoted to the worship of the great Diana and her statue that fell from the sky?
19.36: That being undeniable, you must remain calm and not act rashly.,
19.37: because these men whom you have brought here are neither sacrilegious nor blasphemers of your goddess.
19.38: If Demetrius and those in his industry have a complaint against someone, there are days for hearings and proconsuls: let each one present his grievances.
19.39: If you have any other matters to settle, they will be decided in the legal assembly.
19.40: "We risk being accused of sedition for what happened today, because there is no reason that allows us to justify this gathering." Having spoken thus, he dismissed the assembly.

20.1: When the uproar had ceased, Paul gathered the disciples together, took leave of them, and departed for Macedonia.
20.2: He traveled through that region, giving his disciples many exhortations, and then went to Greece.,
20.3: where he spent three months. He was preparing to embark for Syria when the Jews set traps for him. So he decided to return to Macedonia.
20.4: He was accompanied to Asia by Sopater of Berea, son of Pyrrhus, Aristarchus and Secundus of Thessalonica, Gaius of Derbe, Timothy, Tychicus and Trophimus of Asia.
20.5: They took the lead and waited for us at Troas.
20.6: For us, after the days of Unleavened Bread, we embarked at Philippi and after five days we joined them at Troas, where we spent seven days.
20.7: On the first day of the week, as we were gathered together for the breaking of bread, Paul, who was to leave the next day, spoke with the disciples and prolonged his discourse until midnight.
20.8: There were many lamps in the upper room where we were assembled.
20.9: Now a young man named Eutycus was sitting on the windowsill. During Paul's long speech, he fell into a deep sleep and, overcome by sleep, fell from the third floor; he was found dead.
20.10: But Paul went down, knelt over him, and took him in his arms, saying, «Do not be troubled, for his life is in him.»
20.11: Then, having gone up again, he broke the bread and ate, and he talked for a long time, until daybreak, after which he left.
20.12: As for the young man, he was brought back alive, which was a great source of comfort.
20.13: For us, taking the lead by sea, we embarked for Assos, where we were to pick up Paul, as he had instructed, for he was to make the journey on foot.
20.14: When he joined us at Assos, we took him on board and sailed to Mytilene.
20.15: From there, continuing by sea, we arrived the next day off the coast of Chios. The following day, we arrived at Samos and, [after spending the night at Trogyllus], we arrived the next day at Miletus.
20.16: Paul had decided to pass by Ephesus without stopping, so as not to waste time in Asia. For he was hurrying to be in Jerusalem, if possible, on the day of Pentecost.
20.17: From Miletus, Paul sent to Ephesus to summon the elders of that church.
20.18: When they were gathered around him, he said to them: «You know how, from the first day I set foot in Asia, I have always behaved towards you,
20.19: serving the Lord in all humility, amidst the tears and trials brought about by the snares of the Jews,
20.20: how I have not hidden from you anything that was advantageous to you, not failing to preach and instruct you publicly and in private homes,
20.21: announcing to Jews and Pagans the return to God through penance and faith in Our Lord Jesus Christ.
20.22: And now, bound by the Spirit, I am going to Jerusalem, not knowing what will happen to me there, except that from city to city the Holy Spirit assures me that chains and persecutions await me.
20.24: But I consider it nothing and I value life no more than this, if only I may finish the race and complete the ministry that I received from the Lord Jesus, to proclaim the good news of God’s grace.
20.25: Yes, I know that you will no longer see my face, O all of you among whom I have passed about preaching the kingdom of God.
20.26: That is why I attest to you today that I am pure of all blood.,
20.27: for I have not hidden anything from you the whole plan of God.
20.28: Therefore, take heed to yourselves and to all the flock over which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers, to shepherd the church of the Lord, which he purchased with his own blood.
20.29: I know for certain that after my departure, cruel wolves will enter among you and will not spare the flock.
20.30: And indeed, from among your own number men will arise who will teach perverse doctrines in order to draw away disciples after them.
20.31: Therefore, be watchful, remembering that for three years I did not cease night or day to exhort each one of you with tears.
20.32: And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace, to him who is able to complete the building and to give you the inheritance with all those who are sanctified.
20.33: I have not desired anyone's silver, gold, or clothing.
20.34: You yourselves know that these hands provided for my needs and those of the people who were with me.
20.35: In everything I did, I showed you that by this kind of hard work we must help the weak and remember the words of the Lord Jesus, who himself said, »It is more blessed to give than to receive.”
20.36: After speaking thus, he knelt down and prayed with them all.
20.37: They all burst into tears, throwing themselves into Paul's arms, kissing and embracing him.,
20.38: They were especially distressed by what he had said: "You will never see my face again." And they accompanied him to the ship.

21.1: After tearing ourselves away from their embraces, they embarked and went straight to Kos. The next day we reached Rhodes, then Patara.
21.2: There, having found a boat making the crossing to Phoenicia, we boarded it and set off.
21.3: Having arrived within sight of Cyprus, we left the island to the left, heading towards Syria and landed at Tyre, where the ship was to unload its cargo.
21.4: We found the disciples and stayed there seven days, and they told Paul, by the Spirit of God, not to go up to Jerusalem.
21.5: But after seven days, we made our way to leave, and all of them, with their wives and children, accompanied us outside the city. We knelt on the shore to pray,
21.6: Then, after saying goodbye, we boarded the boat, while they returned home.
21.7: For us, having completed our voyage, we went from Tyre to Ptolemais and having greeted the brothers, we spent a day with them.
21.8: We left the next day and arrived in Caesarea. Having entered the house of Philip the evangelist, one of the seven, we stayed with him.
21.9: He had four virgin daughters, who prophesied.
21.10: As we had been in this city for a few days, a prophet named Agabus arrived from Judea.
21.11: When he came to us, he took Paul's belt, tied his own feet and hands with it, and said, "This is what the Holy Spirit says: The owner of this belt will be bound in Jerusalem by the Jews and handed over to the Gentiles."«
21.12: Having heard these words, we and the faithful of Caesarea urged Paul not to go up to Jerusalem.
21.13: Then he replied, «Why are you weeping like this and breaking my heart? I am ready not only to wear chains, but also to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.»
21.14: Since he remained inflexible, we ceased our entreaties, saying, "The Lord's will be done."«
21.15: After those days, having completed our preparations, we went up to Jerusalem.
21.16: Disciples from Caesarea also came with us, bringing with them a man named Mnason, from the island of Cyprus, a long-time disciple, with whom we were to stay.
21.17: Upon our arrival in Jerusalem, the brothers received us with joy.
21.18: The next day, Paul went with us to Jacques' house and all the Elders gathered there.
21.19: After embracing them, he recounted in detail all that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.
21.20: Having heard this, they glorified God and said to Paul, «You see, brother, how many thousands of Jews have believed, and all are zealous for the Law.
21.21: But they have heard of you that you teach the Jews scattered among the Gentiles to separate themselves from Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children and not to conform to the customs.
21.22: So what to do? Without a doubt, we will gather in a crowd, because we will know of your arrival.
21.23: Do as we tell you. We have four men here who have made a vow,
21.24: Take them, purify yourself with them, and pay for their sacrifices so that they may shave their heads. Then everyone will know that the reports made about you are worthless and that you too are keeping the Law.
21.25: As for the pagans who believed, We wrote to them after We had decided [that they have nothing like it to observe, except] that they must abstain from food offered to idols, blood, strangled animals, and fornication.»
21.26: Then Paul took these men with him and after purifying himself, he entered the temple the next day with them to announce that the days of the Nazirite vow had expired, and he remained there until the sacrifice had been offered for each of them.
21.27: As the seven days were drawing to a close, the Jews of Asia, seeing Paul in the temple, stirred up the whole crowd and seized him, shouting:
21.28: «Children of Israel, help! This is the man who preaches everywhere and to everyone against the people, against the Law, and against this place; he has even brought pagans into the temple and desecrated this holy place.»
21.29: Because they had previously seen Trophimus of Ephesus with him in the city, and they believed that Paul had brought him into the temple.
21.30: Immediately the whole city was in turmoil and the people rushed from all sides, they seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple, whose gates were immediately closed.
21.31: While they were trying to kill him, news reached the tribune of the cohort that all of Jerusalem was in confusion.
21.32: He immediately summoned soldiers and centurions and rushed to them. When they saw the tribune and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
21.33: Then the tribune approached, seized him and had him bound with two chains, then he asked who he was and what he had done.
21.34: But in that crowd, some were shouting one thing, others another. Unable to learn anything for certain because of the tumult, he ordered him to be taken to the fortress.
21.35: When Paul reached the steps of the staircase, he had to be carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the crowd.
21.36: For the crowd followed, shouting, "Kill him!"«
21.37: As he was being brought into the fortress, Paul said to the tribune, "May I say something to you?" "Do you know Greek?" replied the tribune.
21.38: "So you're not the Egyptian who recently revolted and led four thousand assassins into the desert?"»
21.39: Paul said to him, «I am a Jew, from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of a city of considerable renown. Please allow me to speak to the people.»
21.40: Having received permission from the tribune, Paul, standing on the steps, gestured to the people. A profound silence fell, and Paul, speaking in Hebrew, addressed them thus:

22.1: «"My brothers and fathers, listen to what I now have to say in my defense."»
22.2: As soon as they heard him speaking to them in Hebrew, they became even more silent.
22.3: And Paul said, «I am a Jew, born in Tarsus of Cilicia, but brought up in this city and educated at the feet of Gamaliel in the strict knowledge of the Law of our fathers, being zealous for God, as you all are today.
22.4: It was I who persecuted this sect to the death, chaining and imprisoning men and women:
22.5: The high priest and all the elders are witnesses to this. Having even received letters from them for the brothers, I left for Damascus in order to bring those who were there in chains to Jerusalem and to have them punished.
22.6: But as I was on my way and already near Damascus, suddenly, around noon, a bright light from the sky shone around me.
22.7: I fell to the ground and heard a voice saying to me: Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?
22.8: I replied: Who are you, Lord? And he said to me: I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom you are persecuting.
22.9: Those who were with me saw the light, but they did not hear the voice of the one who was speaking to me.
22.10: So I said, “What should I do, Lord?” And the Lord answered me, “Get up, go to Damascus and there you will be told everything you must do.”.
22.11: And as I could no longer see because of the brightness of that light, those who were with me took me by the hand and I arrived in Damascus.
22.12: Now there was a devout man according to the Law, named Ananias, of whom all the Jews in the city spoke well,
22.13: He came to me and, approaching me, said: Saul, my brother, receive your sight. And at that very moment I saw him.
22.14: Then he said: The God of our fathers predestined you to know his will, to see the Righteous One and to hear the words of his mouth.
22.15: For you will be a witness to him before all men of the things that you have seen and heard.
22.16: And now what are you waiting for? Get up, receive baptism and cleanse yourself of your sins, calling on his name.
22.17: Back in Jerusalem, as I was praying in the temple, I happened to be raptured in spirit,
22.18: And I saw the Lord saying to me, “Hurry and leave Jerusalem as quickly as possible, because the testimony that you will give of me will not be accepted there.”.
22.19: “Lord,” I replied, “they themselves know that I imprisoned and beat in the synagogues those who believed in you.”,
22.20: And when the blood of Stephen, your witness, was shed, I myself was present, joining my approval with that of the others, and guarding the garments of those who were stoning him.
22.21: Then he said to me, "Go, I want to send you to distant nations."»
22.22: The Jews had listened to him until these words, then they raised their voices, saying, "Remove such a man from the earth; he is not worthy to live."«
22.23: And as they shouted loudly, throwing off their coats and flinging dust into the air,
22.24: The tribune ordered that Paul be brought into the fortress and tortured with a whip, in order to find out why they were shouting against him.
22.25: The soldiers had already bound him with straps when Paul said to the centurion who was standing there, "Are you allowed to flog a Roman citizen who hasn't even been condemned?"«
22.26: At these words, the centurion went to the tribune to warn him and said, "What are you going to do? This man is a Roman citizen."«
22.27: The tribune came and said to Paul, «Tell me, are you a Roman citizen?» «Yes,» he replied,
22.28: And the tribune replied: "I paid a very high price for this right of citizenship." "And I," said Paul, "have it by birth."«
22.29: Those who were about to torture him immediately withdrew, and the tribune was also afraid when he learned that Paul was a Roman citizen and that he had him bound.
22.30: The next day, wanting to know exactly what the Jews were accusing him of, he had his bonds removed and ordered the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin to assemble, then, having brought Paul down, he placed him in the midst of them.

23.1: Paul, looking intently at the Sanhedrin, said: «My brothers, I have conducted myself before God to this day in all integrity and a good conscience.»
23.2: The high priest Ananias ordered his assistants to strike him on the mouth.
23.3: Then Paul said to him, «Surely God will strike you, whitewashed wall! You sit here to judge me according to the Law, and yet you defy the Law by ordering me to be struck down.»
23.4: The assistants said, "You are insulting the high priest of God."«
23.5: Paul replied, «My brothers, I did not know that he was a high priest, for it is written: »You shall not revile a ruler of your people.’”
23.6: Paul, knowing that part of the assembly was composed of Sadducees and the other part of Pharisees, cried out in the Sanhedrin: «My brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees; it is because of the hope of the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial.»
23.7: As soon as he had spoken these words, a dispute arose between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly was divided.
23.8: For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel and spirit, while the Pharisees affirm both.
23.9: So there was a noisy commotion, and some scribes of the Pharisee party stood up and began a heated debate, saying, «We find no fault in this man if a spirit or an angel had spoken to him?»
23.10: As the discussion grew more heated, the tribune, fearing that Paul would be torn to pieces by them, ordered soldiers to go down and take him away from among them and bring him back to the fortress.
23.11: The following night, the Lord appeared to Paul and said, «Take courage! Just as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, you must also testify about me in Rome.»
23.12: As soon as day broke, the Jews plotted and swore an oath against themselves not to eat or drink until they had killed Paul.
23.13: There were more than forty who had joined this conspiracy.
23.14: They went to the chief priests and the elders and said, «We have solemnly sworn not to eat any food until we have killed Paul.
23.15: "So now you, along with the Sanhedrin, should address the tribune, so that he may bring him before you, as if you wish to examine his case more thoroughly, and we are ready to kill him on the way there."»
23.16: Paul's sister's son, having learned of the plot, rushed to the fortress and informed Paul.
23.17: He called one of the centurions and said to him, "Take this young man to the tribune, for he has something to reveal to him."«
23.18: The centurion, taking the young man with him, led him to the tribune and said, "The prisoner Paul asked me to bring you this young man who has something to tell you."«
23.19: The tribune took him by the hand and, having pulled him aside, asked him: "What do you have to tell me?"«
23.20: He replied: "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul before the Sanhedrin tomorrow, under the pretext of examining his case more thoroughly.
23.21: Don't listen to them, for more than forty of them are lying in wait for him and have sworn, under oaths against themselves, not to eat or drink until they have killed him. They are ready and waiting only for your order.»
23.22: The tribune sent the young man away, after advising him not to tell anyone that he had given him this report.
23.23: And having called two centurions, he said to them, «Have ready, from the third hour of the night, two hundred soldiers with seventy horsemen and two hundred spearmen, to go to Caesarea.
23.24: Also prepare horses for Paul to ride on, so that he can be safely transported to Governor Felix.»
23.25: He had written a letter worded as follows:
23.26: «"Claude Lysias, to the very excellent Governor Felix, greetings.
23.27: The Jews had seized this man and were about to kill him, when I arrived with soldiers and snatched him from their hands, having learned that he was a Roman.
23.28: Wanting to know what crime they were accusing him of, I brought him before their assembly,
23.29: and I found that he was accused concerning matters relating to their law, but had committed no crime that deserved death or imprisonment.
23.30: Having been informed that the Jews were plotting against him, I immediately sent him to you, letting his accusers know that they must explain themselves to you concerning him. [Farewell.]»
23.31: So the soldiers, having taken Paul, according to the order they had received, brought him during the night to Antipatris.
23.32: The next day, leaving the riders to continue on with the prisoner, they returned to the fortress.
23.33: Upon arriving in Caesarea, the horsemen handed the letter to the governor and presented Paul to him.
23.34: The governor, after reading the letter, asked which province Paul was from and, learning that he was from Cilicia:
23.35: «"I will hear you," he said, "when your accusers come," and he ordered him to be kept in Herod's praetorium.

24.1: Five days later, the high priest Ananias arrived, along with some elders and a certain rhetorician named Tertullus; they brought a complaint against Paul to the governor.
24.2: When he was summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him in these terms: «Enjoying profound peace, thanks to you, excellent Felix, and to the reforms that your foresight has brought about in favor of this nation,
24.3: We always and everywhere welcome them with complete gratitude.
24.4: But, so as not to delay you any longer, I ask you to listen to us for a moment with your usual kindness.
24.5: We have found this man: he is a pestilent person, a troublemaker among the Jews throughout the world, a leader of the sect of the Nazarenes,
24.6: and who even tried to desecrate the temple, so we arrested him [and we wanted to judge him according to our law.
24.7: But the tribune Lysias arrived and violently snatched it from our hands.
24.8: and he ordered that his accusers come before you. You yourself will be able, by questioning him, to learn from his own lips all the things of which we accuse him.»
24.9: The Jews joined in this accusation, maintaining that things were this way.
24.10: After the governor had signaled him to speak, Paul replied: «I speak with confidence to defend myself, for I know that you have governed this nation for many years.
24.11: It has been no more than twelve days, you can be sure, since I went up to Jerusalem to worship.
24.12: And I was not seen in the temple speaking to anyone, nor stirring up a crowd, either in the synagogues or in the city,
24.13: and they would not be able to prove what they are now accusing me of.
24.14: I confess to you that I serve the God of our fathers according to the religion they call a sect, believing everything that is written in the Law and the Prophets,
24.15: and having this hope in God, as they themselves have, that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the sinners.
24.16: That is why I too strive to always have a clear conscience before God and men.
24.17: So I came, after several years, to give alms to my compatriots and to present offerings.
24.18: It was then that I was found in the temple, after my consecration, without any crowd or commotion.,
24.19: by certain Jews from Asia, it was up to them to appear before you as accusers, if they had anything to reproach me for.
24.20: Or let them say what crime they found me guilty of when I appeared before the Sanhedrin,
24.21: unless I am accused of a crime for this one word that I spoke aloud in their presence: »It is because of the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial before you today.”
24.22: Felix, who was well acquainted with this religion, adjourned them, saying: "When the tribune Lysias arrives, I will know your case in detail."«
24.23: And he gave orders to the centurion to keep Paul, but allowing him some freedom and not preventing any of his people from serving him.
24.24: A few days later, Felix came with Drusilla, his wife, who was Jewish. Having summoned Paul, he heard him speak about faith in Jesus Christ.
24.25: But when Paul began to speak of justice, chastity, and the coming judgment, Felix, frightened, said, "For now, leave; I will call you back at the first opportunity."«
24.26: At the same time, he hoped that Paul would give him money, so he had him come quite frequently to talk with him.
24.27: Two years passed in this way and Felix was succeeded by Porcius Festus and, in the desire to be pleasing to the Jews, he left Paul in prison.

25.1: Festus, having therefore arrived in his province, went up three days later from Caesarea to Jerusalem.
25.2: The chief priests and the leading Jews came to him to bring charges against Paul. With much earnestness,
25.3: They asked him as a favor, with hostile intent towards the Apostle, to have him transferred to Jerusalem; they planned to ambush him and kill him on the way.
25.4: Festus replied that Paul was being held in Caesarea and that he himself would return there shortly.
25.5: «"Those of you who are qualified to do so," he added, "should come down with me, and if there are charges against this man, let them accuse him."»
25.6: After spending only eight or ten days in Jerusalem, Festus went down to Caesarea. The next day, having taken his place on the judgment seat, he had Paul brought in.
25.7: When he was brought in, the Jews who had come from Jerusalem surrounded him, bringing many serious accusations against him, which they could not prove.
25.8: Paul said in his defense: "I have done nothing wrong, neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar."«
25.9: Festus, wanting to please the Jews, said to Paul, "Do you want to go up to Jerusalem and be tried there before me on these charges?"«
25.10: Paul replied, «I am standing before Caesar’s tribunal, where I must be judged. I have done no wrong to the Jews, as you yourself know very well.”.
25.11: If I have committed some injustice or some crime deserving of death, I do not refuse to die, but if there is no basis for their accusations, no one has the right to hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar.»
25.12: Then Festus, after conferring with his council, replied: "You have appealed to Caesar, you shall go to Caesar."«
25.13: A few days later, King Agrippa and Berenice arrived in Caesarea to greet Festus.
25.14: As they spent several days there, Festus explained Paul's case to the king, saying, "There is a man here whom Felix has left as a prisoner.
25.15: When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews brought charges against him, demanding his condemnation.
25.16: I replied that it is not the custom of the Romans to hand over a man before confronting the accused with his accusers and giving him the means to justify himself against what he is accused of.
25.17: So they came here and, without delay, I took my place the next day on my tribunal and ordered that this man be brought to me.
25.18: When the accusers appeared, they did not accuse him of any of the crimes I suspected.,
25.19: But they had disputes with him concerning their particular religion and a certain Jesus, who died but whom Paul claimed was alive.
25.20: As I was hesitant to investigate these matters, I asked him if he wanted to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on these accusations.
25.21: But when Paul appealed, so that his case might be brought before the emperor, I ordered him to be detained until I could send him to Caesar.»
25.22: Agrippa said to Festus, "I too would have liked to hear this man." "Tomorrow," replied Festus, "you will hear him."«
25.23: The next day, Agrippa and Berenice arrived in great pomp. When they were in the audience hall with the tribunes and the principal people of the city, Paul was brought in by order of Festus.
25.24: And Festus said: «King Agrippa and all of you who are present with us, you have before you the man about whom the Jews have come in large numbers to speak to me either in Jerusalem or here, shouting that he should no longer be allowed to live.
25.25: For my part, having recognized that he had done nothing that deserved death, and having himself appealed to the emperor, I resolved to send him to him.
25.26: Since I have nothing specific to write to the emperor about him, I have summoned him before you, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that after this audience I may draw up my report
25.27: because it seems unreasonable to me to send a prisoner without also stating what he is accused of.»

26.1: Agrippa said to Paul, «You have the floor for your defense.» Then Paul stretched out his hand and gave his defense, saying:
26.2: «I consider myself fortunate, King Agrippa, to have the opportunity today to defend myself before you against all the accusations brought against me by the Jews.”
26.3: Because you know their customs and controversies better than anyone. I therefore ask you to listen to me patiently.
26.4: My life, from the early days of my youth, is known to all the Jews, since it took place in Jerusalem, in the midst of my nation.
26.5: Knowing me for so long, they know, if they wish to bear witness, that I have lived as a Pharisee according to the most austere sect of our religion.
26.6: And now I stand on trial because I have hope in the promise God made to our ancestors,
26.7: a promise whose fulfillment our twelve tribes await, serving God tirelessly, night and day. It is for this hope, O king, that the Jews accuse me.
26.8: Does it therefore seem incredible to you that God resurrects the dead?
26.9: I too had believed that I should oppose with all my might the name of Jesus of Nazareth.
26.10: This is what I did in Jerusalem, I had a great number of saints imprisoned, having received the power from the chief priests, and when they were put to death, I gave my vote.
26.11: Often, going through all the synagogues and waging war against them, I forced them to blaspheme, and my fury growing ever greater, I pursued them even into foreign cities.
26.12: As I was going to Damascus, with full powers and a mandate from the chief priests,
26.13: Around midday, I saw on the road, O king, a light from heaven, brighter than that of the sun, shining around me and my companions.
26.14: We fell to the ground and I heard a voice saying to me in Hebrew: Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It would be hard for you to resist the goad.
26.15: "Who are you, Lord?" I cried. And the Lord said, "I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.".
26.16: But get up and stand firm on your feet, for I have appeared to you in order to appoint you as a servant and witness of the things which you have seen and of those in which I will appear to you again.
26.17: I have taken you from among this people and from the Gentiles to whom I am now sending you,
26.18: to open their eyes, so that they may pass from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, and thus, by faith in me, receive the remission of sins and the inheritance with those who are sanctified.
26.19: Therefore, King Agrippa, I did not resist the heavenly vision,
26.20: But first I preached to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout Judea and among the Gentiles, repentance and conversion to God, through the practice of works worthy of penance.
26.21: That is why the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me.
26.22: It is therefore thanks to God's help that I have remained standing to this day, bearing witness before small and great alike, saying nothing other than what Moses and the prophets foretold,
26.23: to know that Christ had to suffer and that, having risen first from the dead, he would proclaim the light to the people and to the Gentiles.»
26.24: As he spoke thus in his defense, Festus said aloud, "You are talking nonsense, Paul; your great learning is deluding your mind."»
26.25: «I am not being unreasonable, most excellent Festus,” replied Paul, “I am speaking the language of truth and wisdom.
26.26: The king is aware of these things and I speak freely to him about them, convinced that he is fully aware of them, because none of this has happened in secret.
26.27: Do you believe in prophets, King Agrippa? I know you do.»
26.28: Agrippa said to Paul, "You almost persuade me to become a Christian."«
26.29: "Whether by a little or a lot," replied Paul, "would to God that not only you, but also all those who are listening to me right now, were as I am, except for these chains."»
26.30: Then the king stood up, and with him the governor, Berenice, and all their retinue.
26.31: Having withdrawn, they said to one another, "This man has done nothing to deserve death or imprisonment."«
26.32: And Agrippa said to Festus, "He could be released if he hadn't appealed to Caesar."«

27.1: When it was decided that we would go by sea to Italy, Paul and some other prisoners were handed over to a centurion named Julius, of the cohort Augusta.
27.2: We boarded a ship from Adramyttium which was to sail along the coasts of Asia and we set sail, with Aristarchus, a Macedonian from Thessalonica, with us.
27.3: The following day we landed at Sidon and Julius, who treated Paul kindly, allowed him to go to his friends and receive their care.
27.4: Having left from there, we sailed along the coast of Cyprus, because the winds were contrary.
27.5: After crossing the sea that washes the shores of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we arrived at Myra, in Lycia.
27.6: The centurion, having found a ship from Alexandria sailing for Italy, had us board it.
27.7: For several days we sailed slowly, and it was not without difficulty that we reached the latitude of Cnidus, where the wind prevented us from landing. We passed south of the island of Crete, on the side of Salmone,
27.8: and following the coast with difficulty, we arrived at a place called Bons-Ports, near which was the city of Lasaïa.
27.9: A considerable amount of time had passed, and the voyage was becoming dangerous, for the period of fasting was already over. Paul gave this warning to the crew:
27.10: «"My friends," he told them, "I see that the voyage cannot be undertaken without danger and serious damage, not only to the cargo and the ship, but also to our persons."»
27.11: But the centurion had more confidence in what the pilot and the ship's captain said than in Paul's words.
27.12: And as the port was not good for wintering, most were of the opinion to put to sea again and try to reach, to spend the winter, Phoenicia, a port of Crete which faces Africa and Corus.
27.13: A light south wind began to blow, and believing they could execute their plan, they weighed anchor and sailed closer to the coasts of Crete.
27.14: But soon a fierce wind, named Euraquilon, unleashed its fury upon the island.
27.15: The ship was swept away, unable to fight the hurricane, and we let ourselves drift.
27.16: We quickly passed under a small island, named Cauda, and had great difficulty getting the longboat back up.
27.17: When it had been hoisted, the sailors, resorting to all means of salvation, surrounded the ship and, fearing to run aground on the Syrtis, they lowered the sails and let themselves go.
27.18: As we were being violently battered by the storm, the cargo was thrown overboard the next day.,
27.19: and the following day we launched the ship's rigging with our own hands.
27.20: For several days, neither the sun nor the stars appeared and the storm continued to rage violently: all hope of salvation had vanished.
27.21: No one had eaten for a long time. Paul then stood up among them and said to them, «You should have listened to me, my friends, and not left Crete, thus sparing yourselves this peril and loss.
27.22: However, I urge you to take courage, for none of you will lose your life, only the ship will be lost.
27.23: This very night an angel of God, to whom I belong and whom I serve, appeared to me,
27.24: and said to me, Paul, do not be afraid; you must stand trial before Caesar, and behold, God has given you all those who sail with you.
27.25: So take courage, my friends, for I trust in God that it will be as I have been told.
27.26: We must be shipwrecked on an island.»
27.27: On the fourteenth night, as we were being tossed about in the Adriatic, the sailors suspected, around the middle of the night, that we were approaching some land.
27.28: Throwing the probe immediately, they found twenty fathoms, a little further on they threw it again and found fifteen.
27.29: Fearing they would strike reefs, they dropped four anchors from the stern and eagerly awaited daylight.
27.30: But as the sailors were trying to escape from the ship and had already, under the pretext of dropping anchors near the bow, launched the longboat,
27.31: Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers, "If these men do not remain on the ship, you are all lost."«
27.32: Then the soldiers cut the mooring lines of the longboat and let it fall.
27.33: While waiting for the day, Paul urged everyone to eat: «Behold,” he told them, “this is the fourteenth day that you have been anxiously fasting and have not eaten anything.
27.34: I therefore urge you to eat, for this is essential to your salvation; not one of you will lose a hair of your head.»
27.35: Having said this, he took bread and after giving thanks to God in front of everyone, he broke it and began to eat.
27.36: And everyone, regaining their courage, ate too.
27.37: In total, there were two hundred and seventy-six people on the building.
27.38: When they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship by throwing the provisions into the sea.
27.39: When day came, they did not recognize the coast, but having spotted a bay with a sandy beach, they resolved to run the ship aground, if they could.
27.40: So we cut the anchor lines and abandoned them to the sea, at the same time we released the rudder attachments, set the mizzen sail to windward and headed towards the beach.
27.41: But having touched down on a spit of land, they ran aground there, the bow sank and remained motionless, while the stern broke apart under the force of the waves.
27.42: The soldiers were of the opinion that the prisoners should be killed, for fear that one of them might escape by swimming.
27.43: But the centurion, wanting to save Paul, prevented them from carrying out their plan. He ordered those who knew how to swim to jump into the water first and reach land.
27.44: and the others were instructed to stand on planks or on pieces of the ship. And so they all reached the shore safe and sound.

28.1: Once we were rescued, we recognized that the island was called Malta.
28.2: The barbarians treated us with unusual kindness; they gathered us all around a large fire they had lit because of the rain that had come and the cold.
28.3: Paul having gathered some brushwood and thrown it into the fire, a viper, which the heat brought out, attached itself to his hand.
28.4: Seeing the reptile hanging from his hand, the barbarians said to one another: "Without a doubt, this man is a murderer, for after he was saved from the sea, Divine Justice did not want to let him live."«
28.5: He, however, shook the viper into the fire and felt no harm.
28.6: The barbarians expected to see him swell up or suddenly fall dead. But after waiting a long time, seeing that no harm came to him, they changed their minds and said: He is a god.
28.7: In the vicinity, there were lands belonging to the first person of the island, named Publius, who received us and gave us the friendliest hospitality for three days.
28.8: Publius' father was then in bed, sick with fever and dysentery. Paul went to visit him and, after praying, laid his hands on him and healed him.
28.9: Then the other sick people on the island came to him and they were healed.
28.10: We were given great honors upon our departure and were provided with what we needed.
28.11: After a three-month stay, we embarked on a ship from Alexandria which had spent the winter on the island; its ensign was the Dioscuri.
28.12: Having landed at Syracuse, we stayed there for three days.
28.13: From there, following the coast, we reached Reggio and the next day, with the wind blowing from the south, we arrived in two days at Pozzuoli.
28.14: We found some brothers there who asked us to spend seven days with them, then we left for Rome.
28.15: Having heard of our arrival, the brothers from that city came to meet us as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns. When Paul saw them, he gave thanks to God and was filled with confidence.
28.16: When we arrived in Rome, Paul was allowed to have his own lodgings with a soldier guarding him.
28.17: Three days later, Paul summoned the leaders of the Jews, and when they came, he said to them, «My brothers, I have done nothing against the people or against the customs of our fathers, yet I am a prisoner and have been handed over to the Romans from Jerusalem.
28.18: After questioning me, they wanted to release me, because there was nothing in me that deserved death.
28.19: But the Jews opposed it, and I was forced to appeal to Caesar, not that I had any intention of accusing my nation.
28.20: That is why I asked to see you and speak with you, for it is because of the hope of Israel that I wear this chain.»
28.21: They replied, "We have received no letter from Judea concerning you, and none of the brothers who returned from there reported or said anything unfavorable about you.
28.22: But we would like to hear from you what you think, because we know that this sect faces opposition everywhere.»
28.23: Having arranged a day with him, they came in greater numbers to him where he was staying. Paul explained to them, in urgent language, the kingdom of God, seeking to persuade them, from the Law of Moses and the Prophets, concerning Jesus. The discussion lasted from morning until evening.
28.24: Some were convinced by what he said, but others did not believe.
28.25: As they withdrew in disagreement, Paul added only these words: «This is indeed the word that the Holy Spirit spoke to your fathers through the prophet Isaiah:
28.26: Go to this people and say to them: You will hear with your ears but you will not understand, you will see with your eyes but you will not perceive.
28.27: For the heart of this people has become callous, they have hardened their ears and closed their eyes, lest they see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their heart, turn and receive salvation from me.
28.28: Therefore, know that this salvation from God has been sent to the Gentiles; they will receive it with meekness.»
28.29: [When he had spoken thus, the Jews went away, arguing sharply among themselves.]
28.30: Paul remained for two whole years in a house he had rented. He received all those who came to visit him,
28.31: preaching the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ, with all freedom and without hindrance.
